coredata.dc:title: Sustainable use of plastic-derived nanocarbons as a promising larvicidal and growth inhibitor agent towards control of mosquitoes
coredata.dc:creator: Shaw, Vikky
Koley, Rajesh
Das, Sugata
Saha, Tulika
Mondal, Naba Kumar
coredata.dc:description: Nanoscale carbon was obtained from six widely used plastics (PET, HDPE, PVC, LDPE, PP and PP) via thermal degradation (600 °C) under inert atmosphere. The thermally degraded products were processed through bath sonication followed by lyophilisation and the same was characterized through proximate analysis, UV–Vis spectroscopy, Scanning electron micrograph (SEM) with energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis, Transmission electron micrograph (TEM), Dynamic light scattering (DLS) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). A series of aqueous solution of nanoscale carbon (5–30 mg/L) were prepared and same were used as both mosquito growth inhibitor and larvicidal agent against 3rd and 4th instar larvae of Culex pipiens. The significant percent mortality results were recorded for LDPE (p < 0.007) with average particle size of 3.01 nm and 62.95 W% of carbon and PS (p < 0.002) with average particle size of 12.80 nm and 58.73 W% of carbon against 3rd instar larvae, respectively. Similarly, for 4th instar larvae, both significant pupicidal and adulticidal activity were also recorded for PET (F = 24.0, p < 0.0001 and F = 5.73, p < 0.006), and HDPE (F = 26.0, p < 0.0001) and F = 5.30, p < 0.008). However, significant pupicidal activity were observed for PVC (F = 6.90, p < 0.003), and PS (F = 21.30, p < 0.0001). Histological, bio-chemical and microscopic studies were revealed that nanoscale carbon causes mild to severe damage of external and internal cellular integrity of larvae. However, nanoscale carbon does not exhibit any chromosomal abnormality and anatomical irregularities in Allium cepa and Cicer arietinum, respectively. Similarly, non-significant results with respect to blood cell deformation were also recorded from blood smear of Poecilia reticulata. Therefore, it can be concluded that plastic origin nanoscale carbon could be a viable sustainable nano-weapon towards control of insects.

head.ce:title: Sustainable use of plastic-derived nanocarbons as a promising larvicidal and growth inhibitor agent towards control of mosquitoes

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Environmental Chemistry Laboratory, Department of Environmental Science, The University of Burdwan, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Environmental Chemistry Laboratory, Department of Environmental Science, The University of Burdwan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanoscale carbon was obtained from six widely used plastics (PET, HDPE, PVC, LDPE, PP and PP) via thermal degradation (600 °C) under inert atmosphere. The thermally degraded products were processed through bath sonication followed by lyophilisation and the same was characterized through proximate analysis, UV–Vis spectroscopy, Scanning electron micrograph (SEM) with energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis, Transmission electron micrograph (TEM), Dynamic light scattering (DLS) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). A series of aqueous solution of nanoscale carbon (5–30 mg/L) were prepared and same were used as both mosquito growth inhibitor and larvicidal agent against 3rd and 4th instar larvae of Culex pipiens. The significant percent mortality results were recorded for LDPE (p < 0.007) with average particle size of 3.01 nm and 62.95 W% of carbon and PS (p < 0.002) with average particle size of 12.80 nm and 58.73 W% of carbon against 3rd instar larvae, respectively. Similarly, for 4th instar larvae, both significant pupicidal and adulticidal activity were also recorded for PET (F = 24.0, p < 0.0001 and F = 5.73, p < 0.006), and HDPE (F = 26.0, p < 0.0001) and F = 5.30, p < 0.008). However, significant pupicidal activity were observed for PVC (F = 6.90, p < 0.003), and PS (F = 21.30, p < 0.0001). Histological, bio-chemical and microscopic studies were revealed that nanoscale carbon causes mild to severe damage of external and internal cellular integrity of larvae. However, nanoscale carbon does not exhibit any chromosomal abnormality and anatomical irregularities in Allium cepa and Cicer arietinum, respectively. Similarly, non-significant results with respect to blood cell deformation were also recorded from blood smear of Poecilia reticulata. Therefore, it can be concluded that plastic origin nanoscale carbon could be a viable sustainable nano-weapon towards control of insects.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: All relevant data are within the manuscript and its supporting information files.

ce:section.ce:para: It was estimated that around 6.58.3 million ton of plastic wastes per year was generated in India (Aryan et al., 2019). According to World Economic Forum (2023), every year, worldwide 350 million metric tons of plastic waste were produced. Plastics are synthesized under the presence of various plasticisers such as Bisphenol-A, phthalates, dyes etc. which are slowly released from discarded plastic wastes (Wang et al., 2021). Apart from these toxic substances, small sizes plastic particles such as microbeads, resin pellets and microsphere were used in different paints and cosmetics products. These small sizes plastic particles like micro (< 5 mm) and nano plastics (<100 nm) are mainly discharges from different primary and secondary sources, influenced by UV light, wind and water flow etc. (Koelmans et al., 2015). These micro and nano size plastics have enormous pessimistic impact on the environment (Zha et al., 2023; Campanale et al., 2022; Peng et al., 2020; Sun et al., 2018). Therefore, increasing amount of plastic waste subsequently creates hazardous problem to the ecosystem (Fan et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2020). Low priced nonbiodegradable plastic sources further directed to major environmental and health concern (Hahladakis et al., 2018). On other hand, low rate of recyclability has made the single use plastics including plastic bags, cups, bottles, packaging materials along with other nonbiodegradable and biodegradable materials are the major participants of dumping landfills (Ronkay et al., 2021; Ahamed et al., 2019). The improper managements of plastic waste on landfills produces the leaching of chemicals and causes severe environmental pollution of soil, air and ground water (Lin et al., 2023).

Therefore, alternative ways are extremely needed that can be recast to important cherished products including transformation to ecofriendly carbonaceous nano materials (Shaw et al., 2023). Carbonaceous nano materials namely carbon nano particles (CNPs), carbon nanocomposites (CNCs) (FuKai et al., 2022; Park et al., 2011), carbon dots (CDs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs) etc. were frequently used in the current literature (Pierpaoli et al., 2023; Chaudhary et al., 2021; Murugan et al., 2016). Previous researchers (Chaudhary et al., 2021; Kumari and Chaudhary, 2020; Huang et al., 2014) synthesized luminescent carbon dots and carbon quantum dots by using PET plastics. Similarly, carbon dots were also used in the area of electronics as supercapacitors and electrodes (Laforguem et al., 2003) and mosquito controlling agent Sonkar et al., 2012; Saxena et al., 2013).

Mosquito act as a vector of different pathogens and parasites of crucial medical and veterinary aspect (WHO, 2012). The rapid spread of highly aggressive arboviruses, pathogens and parasites along with the development resistance in their mosquito vectors represents a huge challenge in modern vector molecular biology (Chattopadhyay et al., 2022). As per the report from the National Vector Borne Disease Control Programme (NVBDCP, 2023), mosquito is a big threat throughout the India. However, worldwide, millions of peoples are suffering from mosquito borne diseases (Mondal et al., 2022). To dates, several methods, were executed to control mosquito population such as, chemical insecticides (organophosphate, fenitrothion etc.) used as adulticides as well as larvicides, Light trap usually for monitoring, controlled-release of chemical vapour as mosquito repellents, mosquito nets, phytochemicals and several mores throughout the world (Zhang et al., 2021; Banumathi et al., 2017). But none of these techniques are judiciously control mosquito populations. To overcome these challenges, researchers focused on novel and potentials eco-friendly mosquito control tools (Rose et al., 2020; Saha and Gupta, 2017). In last decades, an innovative vector control route, promising interface-based nanotechnology has been developed where main targets are insect larvae. However, nanoparticle synthesis is cost effective technique. Due to biocompatibility, easy to synthesis and cost efficiency of metal nanoparticles such as silver, gold, zinc and others were widely used (Muthukumaran et al., 2015). Very recently, Mondal et al. (2022) reported toxicological effects of AgNPs on different aquatic organism in their mesocosm experiments. Both silver and gold nano particles were showed cytotoxicity on onion root cell which was demonstrated by Debnath et al. (2020) in their research. Thus, metal nanoparticles draw the attention of entomologists towards control of mosquito larva without causing any deleterious effects on non-target aquatic organism. Liu et al. (2009) showed in their research work that fruit fly, exposure with dry forms of carbon black and multiwalled carbon nanotube causes to a strong cohesion and adherence of the nano structure materials and leading to impaired motor functions and insect mortality. Similarly, Saxena et al. (2013) reported that water soluble carbon nanoparticles synthesized from woodwool has the potentiality to arrests the larval growth. In recent past, Raul et al. (2021) also demonstrated vegetable and fruits peel origin carbon dots with silver nano particle hybrids act as potent mosquito larvicidal agent. Almost similar water-soluble carbon nanomaterials were nicely used as growth promotor for gram plant (Sonkar et al., 2012). Hitherto, very scanty information is available which highlighted the effective use of waste plastics towards insect control. From this perspective present work was formulated to convert the waste plastics to carbon nanocomposites materials and their effective utilization as mosquito larvicidal agent to kill the mosquito larvae. On other hand, due to high resistance nature of mosquito larvae, against different chemical insecticides, novel eco-friendly mosquito controlling method are highly desirable. Thus, alternative eco-friendly mosquito controlling methods such as use of carbonaceous nano materials may be one of them.

This experimental work has three distinct parts, first part of the research was focused on the collection, processed and preparation of carbon nanoparticles from different waste plastics (PS: Polystyrene, PVC: Polyvinyl chloride, LDPE: low density polyethylene HDPE: High density polyethylene, PP: Polypropylene and PET: Polyethylene terephthalate). In second part, characterization of carbon nanoparticles by various analytical tools and the efficacy of the synthesized nanocarbon were assessed through mosquito larvicidal activity by determining the LC50 and LC90 value, larval activity, development and behavioural nature upon exposure of Synthesized carbonaceous nano particles were also assessed. In third part of the work was cytotoxicity analysis of non-target organism, and assessed the biochemical stress parameters Cicer arietinum, Allium cepa and Poecilia reticulata.
All the used chemicals were of analytical grade and were utilized without any further purification. They were procured from Merck Pvt. Ltd. (Mumbai) with 99% purity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Collection and purification of waste plastics
ce:section.ce:para: Waste plastics (LDPE, PET, PS, PVC, PP and HDPE,) were collected from the surrounding of The University of Burdwan, West Bengal, India (23.2393° N, 87.8512° E). Collected plastics were thoroughly washed with soap water to remove any dirt and washed twice with distilled water for proper cleaning.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of plastic derived nanocarbonaceous materials (PDNCMs) and preparation of standard aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: The plastic samples were cut into small pieces and were shredded into small flakes/chips of size ranging from 1 to 2 cm. Dried small pieces of plastic was taken in a silica crucible without any catalyst. Then the crucible was put into muffle furnace (Model no. BTL-49, PSG, INDIA) and heat the materials at 600 °C for 1 h (under inert atmosphere by purging nitrogen gas through external cylinder). The black powder like char material was produced. After cooling, the material was grinded by mortar-pestle and sieved, and kept in an air-tight container for future use. Waste plastic origin black powder like char material was dissolved in double distilled water and sonicated in a bath sonicator (Labman- LUCM-3, 100 W, 40 KHz) for 1 h. After sonication, aqueous solution was centrifuges at 9000–10000 rpm for 10 min and supernatant were separated and lyophilized to obtain black powdery material. This powdery like material was collected to make 1000 mg/L stock solution and subsequent dilution of the stock solution was prepared for working standard.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations of synthesized plastic derived nano carbonaceous material

ce:section.ce:para: Synthesized waste plastic origin carbonaceous nanoparticles were characterized by UV–Vis (UV–Vis Spectrophotometer, Optimizen pop, Korea) for initial identification of absorption of carbon nanocomposites. The chemical nature of plastic origin carbon nanoparticles was assessed in the form of available functional groups by fourier infrared spectroscopy (Agilent Cary 630 FTIR, Singapore). XRF were performed through (Bruker ARTAX - ELEMENT ANALYSIS). The Dynamic light scattering (DLS) is used to determine the size and distribution of plastic derived Carbon nanoparticles using the model MALVERN Zetasizer Nano ZS. Microscopic study for understanding the surface morphology and exact shape and size of plastic origin carbon nanoparticles was performed by SEM (SEM, JEOL JSM-6390 LV, USA) (accelerating voltage of 20.0 kV) and EDAX (JEOL JSM-6390 LV, USA) and TEM (TEM, JEOL JEM 1400 plus).

The proximate analysis of six widely used waste plastic derived nanocarbonaceous materials was performed by measuring moisture content, volatile matter, ash content and fixed carbon content by following the standard protocol with slight modification (Shaw et al., 2023). Elemental analysis of synthesized char material after purification was done by help of EDAX (JEOL JSM-6390 LV, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Estimation of carboxylic acid and organic carbon
ce:section.ce:para: The surface groups of the waste plastic derived nanocarbonaceous materials were examined with the help of Boehm titration method (Boehm, 1994) with modification. Organic carbon was measure for each waste plastic derived nanocarbonaceous materials by following standard method of Walkley and Armstrong (1934) with slight modification (Shaw et al., 2023).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Collection and rearing of mosquito larvae
ce:section.ce:para: Larvae of mosquito species were collected from stagnant water bodies of different places of the Hooghly district (Hooghly, West Bengal, India). A mixture of small amount of ground dog biscuit and yeast was providing as larvae food. The pupae were separated and transferred in a glass beaker and placed inside a mosquito rearing cage to retain emerging adults (The larvae were maintained at 27 ± 2C, relative humidity of 75 % ± 5 % with photoperiod cycle of 12 h in the mosquito rearing facility of our laboratory). After emergence, cotton ball soaked with glucose solution was provided for mosquitoes feeding. The taxonomical identification of the larvae was confirmed through gene sequencing and other morphological characters of the obtained culicines by help of an entomologist, (they were identified as Culex pipiens). The mosquitoes were reared with human blood feeding by following the standard method (Hajra et al., 2015). Petri plates with a piece of filter paper and water was placed inside the cage for oviposition and newly hatched eggs were transferred to another beaker and reared until the larvae were grown up to third and fourth instar, laboratory reared 3rd and 4th instar larvae was used for the experiment (Arasu et al., 2019; Hajra and Mondal, 2016).

DNA was extracted from the mosquito by conventional method and ITS region amplification was carried out with ITS primer sequences (ITS1: TCCGTAGGTGAACCTGCGG). The capillary sequencing was done by ABI 3130XL Genetic Analyzer machine as per manufacture's information (Eurofins laboratory, Bangalore, India).

Laboratory reared third and fourth instar mosquito larvae were selected for the larvicidal study. Six different concentrations (5–30 mg/L) of waste plastic derived nano carbonaceous materials were prepared from stock solution (1000 mg/L) in distilled water. Two hundred fifty millilitres of mixed solution were taken in each bowl with a control sets. Twenty mosquito larvae (3rd and 4th instar separately) were taken in each bowl. Simultaneously, three similar experimental setups were also run as replica (Sarma et al., 2019; WHO, 2005). The entire experiment was performed in mosquito rearing room with well-ventilated and proper lighting arrangement.

Different concentrations of synthesized waste plastic derived nano carbonaceous materials aqueous solutions were evaluated for the bioassay. The numbers of dead larvae were counted after 48 h of exposures, and the percentage mortality was calculated by using Abbott's formula (Abbott, 1925).
Percent Mortality =
N 0
N Total
× 100
[N0 = Number of dead larvae, N total = Number of larvae introduce].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pupal and adulticidal activities of mosquito larvae
ce:section.ce:para: Both pupal and adulticidal activities were assessed through the observation under different concentration (5–30 mg/L) of waste plastic derived nano carbonaceous particles solution. After treatment period, transforming pupa and adults were counted after 96 h and behavioural activity with growth pattern were also analysed throughout exposure time period.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of plastic derived nanocarbonaceous materials on larval behaviour
ce:section.ce:para: A total of 20, Culex pipiens larvae from third and fourth instar were exposed to PDNCMs (nano carbon solution where 50 % larvae population were reduced) solution separately. The larvae were observed at specific time intervals of 1, 5, 9 h, and 24 h followed by final observations at 48 h. The changes in their behaviour pattern such as vertical or horizontal movements, wriggling, excitation, agitation, aggression, aggregation, inability to rise up to the water surface, unnatural positions, uncertain movements, and convulsions were observed and recorded.

The nutritional status of the treatment larvae compare with control larvae was assessed (conc. Where 50% larvae population were reduced), assuming that exposure to waste plastic derived nano carbonaceous material (PDNCMs) aqueous solution could induce disruptions in energy metabolism. The total protein content of control and experimental larval homogenates were estimated through Lowry method (Lowry et al., 1951). Carbohydrate was measured according to van Handel (Van Handel, 1984). Total lipids were estimated by Bragdon's method (Bragdon, 1951) and Glycogen content was assessed according standard method Mondal et al. (2022).

The acetylcholine esterase activity was determined in the larval homogenate according to Ellman et al. (1961) with slight modification (Parthiban et al., 2019), using acetylcholine iodide as a substrate. About 50 μL of supernatant was dissolved in 850 μL of 100 mM sodium phosphate buffer (pH 7.5). To each reaction mixture, 50 μL of 10 mM DTNB and 50 μL of 12.5 mM acetylcholine iodide were added and incubated at room temperature for 5–10 min. After that optical density was read at 405 nm.

X-ray fluorescence study was performed to analysed the presence of different elements in treated larvae as well as in plastic derived nanocarbon. Firstly, isolate the larvae, soaked excess water through blotting paper, dried and grounded in mortar pestle to made powder form. These powder form of treatment larvae were subjected to XRF analysis (Bruker ARTAX - ELEMENT ANALYSIS) (Method: Standard (Bayes), Count rate: 1249 cps, Voltage: 50 kV, Anode: Mo, Optic: Collimator 0.650, Live time: 60 s, Dead time: 0.3 %, Current: 698 μA, Filter: No filter, Atmosphere: Air).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microscopic based morphological study of larvae
ce:section.ce:para: Both instar larvae were exposed to the (nano carbon solution where 50 % larvae population were reduced) PDNCMs solution separately for 24 h, 20 larvae in every treatment set with three replicates. Dead and alive larvae were observed and examined under a stereo light Microscope (Olympus SZ51) and PC attached bright field microscope (Zeiss Axiolab 5) for morphological analysis. The morphological changes in different body part such as, the external organs, abdomen, head, thorax, and mouth brushes, eyes, antennae, setae, siphon, saddle and anal papillae, were examined. Body pigmentation or any other visible aberrations were also examined along with comparison between treated larvae and control larvae.

Treated third and fourth instar larvae from (conc. where 50 % larvae population were reduced) six different nanocarbon solutions were isolated after 48 h of exposure and kept for examination. Then, they were passed through the series of ethanol grade (50 %, 70 %, 90 %, and 100 %) for dehydration, passed through xylene, fixed in paraffin wax. Tissues (cross sections) were sectioned at 5 μm using Leica rotary microtome and stained with haematoxylin and eosin. Larvae from control set were also look over in the same manner. Stained sections of treated and control larvae were observed and photographed under a microscope (Zeiss Axiolab 5) to analyse the microscopic changes in the gut morphology.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Germination and stress parameters study of Cicer arietinum (Gram Seed)
ce:section.ce:para: Effect of waste plastic derived nanocarbon particles on Cicer arietinum germination were analysed to study, how carbon nano particles interact with gram seed cell, and also analysed the role of different stress parameters on exposure of carbon nanoparticle solutions (nano carbon solution where 50 % larvae population were reduced) on germination of gram seed. Twenty healthy seeds were soaked overnight in distilled water followed by surface sterilization was made with 1 % solution of sodium hypo-chloride and germination study were done with three replicates. The entire experiment was performed at room temperature 25 ± 1 °C and humidity range was 70–75 %. All the replicates were illuminated within daylight with a photoperiod of 16/8 h (light/dark). Total observation period was twenty-one days and the germination was counted after 24 h, 48 h and 72 h. Different stress and biochemical parameter were analysed during the time period between 14 and 21 days.

For germination study, both control and treated seeds were counted after 24 h, 48 h and 72 h time period until all the seeds were germinated. Fresh weight (FW) and dry weight (DW) of all treatment sets were recorded before starting the experiment. To measure dry weight, plants were dried at 80 °C up to constant weight (usually 24 h) (Medda and Mondal, 2017).

Plant samples were taken from treated and Control sets for carbohydrate and protein estimation. Carbohydrate was estimated by following the modified standard protocol (Hedge and Hofreiter, 1962). Protein was measured according to Lowry method (Lowry et al., 1951).

Both chlorophyll and carotenoids were measured by following the standard protocols (Arnon, 1949; Maclachan and Zalik, 1963). leave samples were washed, cut into small pieces. Taken 0.1 g leaves and smash into 80 % cold acetone and kept 5 min. After that, the sample solutions were measured at 450 nm, 510 nm, 645 nm, 652 nm and 663 nm wavelength. Estimation of pigments was calculated by use of the following equations. The concentration of the pigment expressed as mg/g F·W
Chl ‘

mg g

=
12.7 × D 663 − 2.69 × D 645
× V / 1000 × W

Carotenoid content

=
7.6 × D 480 – 1.49 × D 510
× V / 1000 × W
where, D = optical density, V = final volume of 80 % acetone, W = weight of the sample, f.w = fresh weight of the plant sample.

Fresh leaves (0.1 g) were taken and properly homogenized using 3 % sulfosalicylic acid. The homogenates were filtered through filter paper. 2 mL each of the filtrate, glacial acetic acid and ninhydrin was mixed together in test tube and incubated at 100C for 1 h (Bates et al., 1973). After cooling in ice bucket, test tubes were removed and toluene (2 mL) was added and the mixture was vigorously shaken for a few seconds. The separated aqueous layer of toluene was warmed at room temperature; the coloured sample was measured at 520 nm wavelength.

MDA content was estimated according to standard method Mondal (2017) with slight modification. Tissue (0.1 mg) was homogenized by adding 10 mL of 0.1 % (w/v) TCA. The homogenate was centrifuged for 10 min (14,000 rpm at 4 °C). 1 mL of supernatant was mixed with 4 mL of 0.5 % ΤВА diluted in 20 % TCA and incubated in water bath at 95 °C for 25–30 min. The reaction was stopped by cooling on ice. Absorbance was measured and recorded at 532 nm and 600 nm of MDA content calculated by using the following formula in sample (Heath and Packer, 1968).
MDA
equivalents
nmol
cm − 1
= 1000
Abs 532 – Abs 600
/ 155

Activity of catalase were determined from both control and treated plant samples by following the standard protocol (Sinha, 1972). The activity was expressed as μm H2O2/g protein/min (Tanase et al., 2013).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Histological assessment of xylem and phloem of Cicer arietinum
ce:section.ce:para: To analyse the anatomical structure of non-target seedling, both root and shoot of seedlings were treated with the nano carbon solution where 50 % larvae population were reduced (Table 2). Transverse sections of both xylem and phloem were made manually. The transverse section was stained with safranin and temporary slides were prepared. The stained sections were examined and photographed under a bright field microscope (Zeiss Axiolab 5).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity and chromosomal study of Allium cepa root tip
ce:section.ce:para:
The cytotoxicity of different waste plastic derived nano carbonaceous materials (PDNCMs) was performed on mitotic cells of Allium cepa root. Initially, onion bulbs were grown on wet sand until the roots were achieved of about 2–4 cm in length. Then the bulbs were transferred into glass jar containing different PDNCMs solutions (nano carbon solution where 50 % larvae population were reduced) and distilled water (control) and treated for 3 days and 7 days separately in two experimental sets to determine cytotoxicity on both above-mentioned time periods. The treated root tips were then excised and fixed with Carnoy's fluid and stored in 70 % ethanol at 4 °C for further study. For microscopic observation, root tips were stained with aceto-orcein (2 %) and squashed on clean slides and observed under bright field microscope (Zeiss Axiolab 5 microscope). For each treatment about 1000 cells were counted (250–300 cells per slide) in triplicates and different mitotic phases and chromosomal abnormalities were recorded. Further, mitotic index, abnormality index, phase index (Saha and Gupta, 2017) and percentage of specific abnormality (Fatma et al., 2017) were calculated using following formulae.
Mitotic index

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feeding potential, behavioural nature, amount of muscle glucose and protein status, blood smear of Poecilia reticulata after feeding of PDNCMs treated larvae
ce:section.ce:para: Adult females of the Poecilia reticulata (guppy fish) were brought from local fish market and acclimatized during three days in laboratory conditions in aquaria of 30 L capacity. After that fishes were transfer in seven separate fish bowl of 3 L water capacity (five fishes in each fish bowl). During the period of experimentation (10 days), having three experimental groups as replica, the treated group of fishes were fed with PDNCMs treated larvae and control group of fish was fed with normal untreated mosquito larvae(III and IV instar), on an average of five larvae for each individual of all set of fishes every day Cleaning, dewatering, re-exchange with freshwater were maintained on every alternate day to avoid water contamination and aeration was provided to meet up sufficient dissolved oxygen. The behavioural changes, e.g., movement of each fish, feeding intensity, amount of excretory generated, occurrence of any diseases etc. were observed vividly and finally, at the end of experiment three fresh and healthy fishes were randomly selected and sacrificed for getting the target tissue, i.e., muscle tissue. Glucose estimation performed by following the standard method of Das et al. (2021) with slight modification and protein were measured through Lowry method (Lowry et al., 1951). Air-dried blood smear was stained by hematoxiline-Eosin and Giemsa stain for observations of erythrocytes through Leica DM 2000 microscope.

Experimental data were analysed by using computerized software model. The entire experimental data were statistically analysed. The median lethal concentration, All the results were presented as mean ± SD. The variation of different concentrations was analysed through one way ANOVA test with the probability level of 0.05. χ2, and other statistical methods were performed by using SPSS, 26 and Minitab 19. Graphical representations and calculations were performed by Origin Pro 21 and MS-Excel.

UV–vis spectroscopy was executed to investigate the absorption nature of the carbonaceous nanomaterials in the range of 200–600 nm wavelength. Fig. 1(A) demonstrated the sharp absorption peaks ranges between the 260–280 nm wavelengths for all six nanocarbon materials synthesized from waste plastics. Fig. 1A clearly demonstrated the sharp absorption peaks at 260 nm for PET, 270 nm for both HDPE and LDPE, and 280 nm for PVC, PP, and PS, respectively. It is interesting to note that, waste plastic origin nanocarbon laden solution with double distilled water showed characteristic absorption peaks from 260 nm to 280 nm. Chaudhary et al. (2021) reported the similar sharp peaks of carbon nanoparticles at 250–360 nm. These sharp peaks could be the existence of various bonds such as -C=O, -C-OH, CC and -C=C- that representing electronic transition in the sp2 hybridization of synthesized carbonaceous nanomaterials for the existence of hydroxyl (-OH) and carboxylic (-COOH) group on the surface of carbon nano material. Almost similar characteristic peaks were also observed in our current study.

The band gaps were calculated from Tauc plot (Fig. S7) and the values are 4.65 eV, 4.09 eV, 4.16 eV, 4.19 eV, 4.33 eV and 3.94 eV for PET, HDPE, PVC, LDPE, PP and PS, respectively. PET and PS were exhibit their absorption spectra in lower and higher wavelength, respectively and this variation in the values is directly related with the sizes and shapes of prepared nanocarbon particles. These variable band gap of different polymer origin carbons are due to different sizes and shapes of nanocarbons (Kumari and Chaudhary, 2020).

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) of all six-nanoscale carbon were performed to ascertain and analysed the existing of various functional groups on their surface and same was demonstrated in Fig. 1(B). The characteristics IR signature were recorded at 3406 cm−1, 3138 cm−1, 2281 cm−1, 1625 cm−1, 1401 cm−1, 1110 cm−1, 879 cm−1, 820 cm−1 and 752 cm−1 from PET plastic origin nanocarbon materials which corresponds the functional group as alcoholic -OH, hydrogen bonded -NH, hydrogen, -CH=CH (trans), OH of carboxylic acid, -C=C, ester -C=O/O-CH2 stretching and ring CC bending. HDPE origin nanocarbon showed infrared spectral signature at 3399 cm−1, 3145 cm−1, 2281 cm−1, 1625 cm−1, 1401 cm−1, 1021 cm−1 and 670 cm−1 which represents corresponding functional groups as alcoholic -OH, hydrogen bonded -NH, carboxyl = C=O, -C=C-, strong CC bending alkene, respectively. Almost similar spectral signature of HDPE origin carbon nano material was reported previously (Hu et al., 2019). It is interesting, to note that the IR signature obtained from PVC - origin nanocarbon is different and most promising signatures were at 3421 cm−1, 3138 cm−1, 2251 cm−1, 1632 cm−1, 1401 cm−1, 879 cm−1 and 715 cm−1 which corresponds the functional groups like alcoholic -OH, hydrogen bonded -NH, hydrogen, -CH=CH (trans), OH of carboxylic acid, -C=C, ester -C=O/O-CH2 stretching and ring CC bending functional groups, respectively. Among the sharp peaks at 3421 cm−1,1632 cm−1, 879 cm−1 likely to special IR spectra of PVC (Xu et al., 2018). The sharp absorption peak at 3300–3270 cm−1 indicates presence of ≡CH stretching of alkynes and strong peak at 879 cm−1 corresponds to asymmetrical stretching of -C-Cl, previous studies (Xu et al., 2018; Shaw et al., 2023) also supported the present findings. The characteristic spectral signatures were obtained from LDPE at 3138 cm−1, 2383 cm−1, 1632 cm−1, 1401 cm−1, 1021 cm−1, and 670 cm−1 which are very similar with HDPE. Polypropylene shows stretching and vibration at 3318 cm−1, 3161 cm−1, 2283 cm−1, 1637 cm−1, 1401 cm−1, 1118 cm−1, and 752 cm−1 which corresponds the functional group of -OH stretching vibration, hydrogen bonded alcohol, carboxylic acid groups, respectively. These findings are exactly supported with our recent research (Shaw et al., 2023). Polystyrene origin nanocarbon showed IR signatures at 3131 cm−1, 2519 cm−1, 2370 cm−1, 1796 cm−1, 1401 cm−1, 1118 cm−1 which represent the functional group as hydrogen bonded -NH, strong broad -OH stretching carboxylic acid, strong CO stretching, -CH=CH, Carboxyl = C=O, -C=C-, and secondary amine.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proximate, elemental and chemical analysis of waste plastic origin carbonaceous char
ce:section.ce:para: Table 1 . represents the proximate analysis results of nanocarbon obtained from six widely used waste plastic. The estimated volatile matter was ranges from 87.98 % to 93.85 % for all six- plastic origin nanocarbon. Whereas, moisture and ash content were ranging between 0.30 and 0.87 % and 1.09–2.28 % respectively. Similarly, fixed carbon was also recorded as 3.42 % to 9.79 % for all six-polymer origin nanocarbon materials. Earlier research report also in accordance with present findings (Jamradloedluka and Lertsatitthanakornb, 2014). The elemental compositions of six plastics origin nanocarbon was also presented in Table 1. From this table it is clear that LDPE origin nanocarbon has maximum carbon content (62.95 %) (Singh et al., 2019) followed by PS (58.73 %) and PP (41.59 %) and very low level of carbon was recorded in PVC (16.04 %). Which again constituted high level of chlorine (56.67 %). Similarly, percentage oxygen and nitrogen were ranges from 14.45 to 44.77 % and 2.80 to 19.56 % in six waste plastic origin nanocarbon, respectively. In present study, we did not record any hydrogen signature in EDAX study. However, previous literatures reported the existence of hydrogen in plastic origin carbon (Singh and Ruj, 2016). On other hand, presence of carboxylic acid group was assessed through acid-base titration method (Saxena et al., 2013). The amount of carboxylic group ranges between 7.23 μ mole to 9.26 μ mole per gram of carbonaceous char materials were recorded from all the six-polymer origin nanocarbon. This information supported the possible solubility of nanocarbon in aqueous medium (Sonkar et al., 2012). Again, the percentage of organic carbon was recorded as 43 % for PET, 38.5 % for HDPE, 33.2 % for PVC, 24.05 % for LDPE, 45.86 % for PP and 41.28 % for PS, respectively (Table 1). In current literature Zheng et al. (2023) demonstrated such higher percentage of total organic carbon in micro and nano plastics.

Scanning electron microscope (SEM) with Energy-dispersive X-ray analysis (EDAX) is an extensively utilized process for screening and identifying the size, shape and distribution with elemental composition of nanomaterials. SEM images of synthesized carbon nanomaterials with different magnification are depicted in Fig. 2 . Analysis revealed a cluster type interconnected pattern of nanocarbon, which are attached as the result of weak van der wall's force and electrostatic charges of attraction (Korde et al., 2020). The average size of the nanocarbon particles was recorded <100 nm and suggested assembled morphology draw chain of carbon nano sphere type shapes. PET origin carbon nano are irregular size and shapes with shoot like appearance. This shoot like porous irregular shaped is due to the effect of sintering under high temperature carbonization (Shaw et al., 2023; Ahangar et al., 2021). Kaur and Pavia (2020) were demonstrated almost similar type of results from pet plastic origin porous and rough carbon nanomaterials by SEM study. HDPE carbon nano is mostly spherical in shape and porous in nature with <100 nm in size. PVC origin carbon showed spherical shaped with layered surface morphology. LDPE and Polypropylene origin carbon showed porous and spherical shaped nanocarbon with almost uniform distribution. On the other hand, Polystyrene derived carbon showed vast heterogenous surface with layered structure appearance. Similar surface morphology of waste plastic origin carbon was reported by Hussin et al. (2021). Yaqoob et al. (2022) also suggested that transformation of waste plastic to carbonaceous materials like CNTs, CQDs, CNCs, activated and porous carbon are extremely important. Elemental analysis of synthesized nanocarbon through EDAX was evaluated to explore the weight percentage carbon in all six nanocarbon. EDAX results showed that the maximum (62.95 %) and minimum (16.04 %) weight % of carbon in LDPE and PVC, respectively. However, moderate level of weight percentage carbon was also recorded as 58.73 %. 41.59 %, 98.68 % and 30.21 % for PS, PP, HDPE and PET, respectively.

Thermally calcinated and purified nanocarbon was synthesized from six widely used plastic waste and same were subjected to transmission electron micrograph and the results are represented in Fig. 3 . Fig. 3 indicates that nanocarbon prepared from six different plastic precursors were exhibited dissimilar shapes and sizes. In which PET and LDPE origin nanocarbon are uniform in shapes and sizes with spherical appearance and average particle size was recorded as 1.06 nm and 3.01 nm for PET and LDPE, respectively. HDPE origin nanocarbon shows almost uniform particle size (3.78 nm) with spherical appearance. Particles obtained from PVC, PP and PS are divergent in size and shapes. However, the average particle size of PVC, PP and PS origin nanocarbon particles were recorded as 7.62 nm, 18.14 nm, and 12.80 nm, respectively (Fig. S6). Mishra et al. (2012) successfully converted the waste polypropylene to multiwall carbon nano composites by using nickel catalysts. Similarly, Yao et al. (2022) also reported the synthesis and effective use of nanocarbon particle from plastic wastes. Similar type of conversion from plastics waste to nanocarbon materials was also reported by previous author Yang et al. (2014).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) study of plastic derived nanocarbon
ce:section.ce:para: DLS analyses were conducted to determine the hydrodynamic size of the particles and their aggregation. The powdered nanocarbon was easily dispersed in water, and their aqueous solutions were used to measure the particle size (Fig. S9). PET plastic derives nanocarbon showed two distinct peaks and the hydrodynamic particle diameter are broad and ranges 141.8 ± 21.40 nm to 531.2 ± 13.7 nm. However, HDPE and PVC derived carbon nano particles displayed similar type of hydrodynamic diameter with average size of 531.2 ± 147.3 nm and 531.2 ± 113.2 nm, respectively. LDPE origin carbon nano particles showed the hydrodynamic particle diameter of 78.82 ± 0.0 nm. PP derived nanocarbon particles has almost similar hydrodynamic diameter (141.8 ± 9.35 nm) like PET origin carbon nano. On the other hand, PS origin nanocarbon exhibited hydrodynamic diameter of 396.1 ± 31.24 nm. The DLS data of all six-waste plastic derived nanocarbon showed broad hydrodynamic diameter of particles along with polydispersity nature. These broad size distribution and larger particle size mask or hide the presence of small carbon particle in their size distribution. Comparing with TEM observation (particle size ranges between 1 and 18 nm), DLS results showed broad size distribution, because it considers overall hydrodynamic diameter that include particle size, as well as adsorbed molecule and ions (Arízaga et al., 2022; Ray et al., 2009).

Molecular identification of target mosquito species with help of ITS1 study has revealed that collected mosquito samples were Culex pipiens as the ITS1 of collected samples shows highest similarity with this species. NCBI gene blasting data shows that the collected mosquito species is Culex pipiens (Fig. S1).

Synthesized waste plastic derived nanocarbon aqueous solutions showed a significant mortality status for both third and fourth instar larvae upon exposure at different concentration (5 mg/L, 10 mg/L, 15 mg/L, 20 mg/L, 25 mg/L, and 30 mg/L) and retarded developmental transformations of Culex larvae are represented in Table 2 and Table 3 , respectively. The average mortality status of third and 4th instar larvae of three replicates of all treatments were represented as % mortality, χ2 and p-value (Table 2). The results indicate, that the concentration where 50 % larvae population were reduced for 3rd instar larvae follows PET (31.50 mg/L) < HDPE (35.17 mg/L) < PS (38.40 mg/L) < LDPE (40.28 mg/L) < PP (53.46 mg/L) < PVC (59.85 mg/L). similarly, for 4th instar larvae, follows the sequence as LDPE (37.05 mg/L) < PS (41.70 mg/L) < PP (42.84 mg/L) < PET (42.99 mg/L) < HDPE (46.63 mg/L) < PVC (48.09 mg/L). Percent mortality of third and fourth instar larvae at lower concentrations (5 and 10 mg/L) of all PDNCMs showed negligible results except PP and PS (Table 2), Where only 10 % mortality was achieved at 10 mg/L concentration. However, the mean mortality at higher concentrations of third and 4th instar larvae were recorded as 40 % and 25 % for PET, 35 % and 25 % for HDPE, 25 % and 30 % for PVC, 30 % and 35 % for LDPE, 40 % and 40 % for PP and 45 % and 40 % for PS, respectively. Therefore, the higher level of mortality for 3rd instar larvae was recorded from PP and PS origin nanocarbon solution. Whereas, for 4th instar larvae, maximum 40 % mortality for both PP and PS were recorded at higher concentration range. Finally, the statistical calculation revelled a significant difference between the concentration where 50% and 90 % larvae population were reduced from third and fourth instar larvae for LDPE (χ2–13.992, p < 0.007) and PS (χ2–16.606, p < 0.002). For 4th instar larvae only, PP showed very marginal non-significant result (χ2–8.808, p < 0.066). Almost similar concentration dependent mortality was reported by the earlier reporter using nanocarbon against the Culex quinquefasciatus larvae (Shaw et al., 2023; Mondal et al., 2022; Murugan et al., 2015).

Waste plastic derived nanocarbon were significantly affected larval growth and observed results (Table 3) suggested that all six nanocarbon treated solution has notable role towards larval growth arrest and delayed transformation from larvae to pupa and pupa to adult compare to control. Table 3 demonstrated the entire larval growth and developmental report under different treatment and time interval. From the Table 3 it is clear that larval growth was significantly arrested and transformation from larvae- pupa-adult were delayed. Moreover, this growth retardation is completely concentration dependent. PDNCMs could damage the larval chitin layer along with mild to severe damage of alimentary canal (Fig. S2). This damage could be the reasons of malfunctioning of protein and hormones and ultimately impacted on growth retardation (Gunathilaka et al., 2021; Sultana et al., 2020). However, the role of microbiota towards the development of larvae cannot be rouledout. In this study, different doses of PDNCMs exhibited mild to severe damage of alimentary canals which directly influenced on the population of microbiota and subsequently larval growth retardation. Moreover, due to substantial damage of alimentary canal under influence of nanocarbon, it directly influenced on food intake of larvae and ultimately causes a sever crisis on energy storage (Sharma et al., 2011). Study results also revelled a gradual decrease of protein level, retarded hormonal activity, delayed transformation etc. with increasing the dose of PDNCMs which also support the earlier observations (Shaw et al., 2023; Saxena et al., 2013).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Larval behavioural study under PDNCMs exposure
ce:section.ce:para: 24 h of exposure under Concentration value where 50 % larvae population were reduced of all six PDNCMs aqueous solution exhibited remarkable behavioural changes of both third and fourth instar larvae (Table 2). The information's from Fig. S2-S3 and Table 2, it is clear that above dose affects larval normal wriggling movements. These is possibly due to negative effects of nanocarbon upon interaction with larval body surface, siphon, caudal and body hairs, mouth brushes and with alimentary canal etc. caused to slow down larval excitation and normal behaviour. Moreover, Fig. S2-S3 clearly reflected that nanocarbon caused cellular damage of larvae and it may induce alteration of larval normal and feeding behaviour, However, control larvae does not exhibit such type of behavioural changes.

Both third and fourth instar Culex pipiens larvae were exposed with the Concentration value where 50 % larvae population were reduced in PDNCMs solutions and the biochemical analysis of exposed larvae were presented in Fig. 4 and Table S1. The results showed that the carbohydrate level of 3rd instar larvae was reduced to 17.69 % and 21.10 % with respect to control under the influence of HDPE and PVC origin nanocarbon. However, treated larvae from PET, LDPE, PP and PS origin nanocarbon showed 9.57 %, 10.55 %, 15.25 % and 1.13 % increased amount of carbohydrate level compared to control. On other hand, treated larvae of 4th instar from all six-polymer origin nanocarbon exposure exhibited 34.8 %, 67.29 %, 52.2 %, 76.31 %, 41.09 % and 57.23 %, reduction of carbohydrate level. Mondal et al. (2022) demonstrated that plant extract mediated nano particles induced oxidative stress and unfavourable condition in larval body, and that caused unable to larvae to feed, that might increase the glycogenolysis, and caused hyperglycaemia (Sharma et al., 2011). The reduction of carbohydrate is due to utilization of reserved food during metabolism. Fig. S2-S3 indicates all larvae with more or less damaged alimentary canal during the exposure of carbonaceous nano materials and treated larvae with highly damaged alimentary canal could not able to proper assimilation of food and subsequently increased the carbohydrate level, and those larvae with less damaged and blocked alimentary canal might be inhibited the larvae from feeding and lowers the glucose level (Sharma et al., 2011).

Similarly, both instar larvae showed reduction trends of protein content in larvae under the exposure of nanocarbon (Fig. 4). The reduction of protein level is likely due to direct interference of polymer origin nanocarbon on protein which regulate hormone synthesis and directly affected larval transformation (Saxena et al., 2013). Small sizes, nanocarbon particles could be able to penetrate the cellular membrane without any obstructions and lead to severe damage of chitin layer (Shaw et al., 2023; Hajra and Mondal, 2016). Details deformations of chitin layer along with intracellular protein demonstrated in Fig. S2-S3.

The impact of plastic origin nanocarbon on the both third and fourth instar larvae exhibited variable results with respect to lipid level (Fig. 4). Both increase and decreased level of lipid amount was recorded against both 3rd and 4th instar larvae. These upregulation and down regulation of lipid level are probably due to improper lipid metabolism in the larval body. Increase level of lipid probably due to alteration in lipid peroxidation rate during the detoxification in larval metabolism. However, the declined trend of total lipid level in larval body is possibly due to the generation of oxidative stress under influence of nanocarbon and this stress may negatively be impacted on lipid metabolism and peroxidation (Sharma et al., 2011; Senthilkumar et al., 2009).

Almost similar decline trend of glycogen for both third and fourth instar larvae under the influence of polymer origin nanocarbon was recorded (Fig. 4). This lowering trend of glycogen is due to oxidation of pyruvate to acetyl coA under interaction of nano particles (Mondal et al., 2022; Hu et al., 2011).

Acetylcholine esterase (AchE) is an important neurotransmitter biomolecule that play a pivotal role at synaptic cleft where it binds to receptor and mediates sodium ion movement through channels (Mondal et al., 2022). AchE also play a role to convert acetylcholine to acetate and choline in synaptic cleft and return back the choline to presynaptic terminal to take part again in synthesis mechanism along with acetyl CoA (Parthiban et al., 2019). In present study the amount of acetylcholine esterase (AchE) in control larvae was recorded 2.06 μm/mg/min. However, nanocarbon treated larvae showed declining trend of AchE. The percent reduction of AchE of 3rd instar larvae was recorded as 15.62 %, 8.85 %, 4.68 %, 14.06 %, 11.65 % and 7.29 % from the exposure of nanocarbon obtained from PET, HDPE, PVC, LDPE, PP and PS, respectively (Fig. 4A, Table S1). Similarly for 4th instar larvae, the percent reduction of AchE was recorded as 11.17 %, 7.28 %, 11.65 %, 15.04 %, 13.59 % and 3.39 % for the polymer origin nanocarbon obtained from PET, HDPE, PVC, LDPE, PP and PS, respectively (Fig. 4B, Table S1). The reduction trend of AchE under the interaction of nanocarbon is possibly due to the blockage of larval nervous system by inhibiting voltage gated ion channels or directly inhibiting the essential enzymes synthesis (Knutsson et al., 2018) by these polymer origin carbon and subsequently release of AchE was hampered compared to control larvae. Moreover, the shortage of AchE leads to excessive accumulation of acetylcholine at presynaptic junctions activated sodium channel allows uninterrupted passing of Na+ which subsequently create a strong stimulus. Therefore, nanocarbon could be serving as a disturbing substance against Culex pipiens larvae (Oehmichen and Besserer, 1982; Mondal et al., 2022).

ce:section.ce:section-title: XRF- (X-ray fluorescence) study of dead larvae
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized nanocarbon from six widely used polymers (HDPE, PVC, LDPE, PS, PET, and PP) were exposed to the third and fourth instar Culex larvae were subjected to X-ray fluorescence study and the results are demonstrated in Fig. S4. From Fig. S4, it is clear that none of the carbonaceous char derived from waste plastic showed the existence of phosphorous and sulphur in raw polymer origin carbon. However, polymer origin nanocarbon (HDPE, PVC, LDPE and PS) treated larvae exhibited the existent of sharp peaks of phosphorous and sulphur. However, PET and PP origin treated larvae only exhibited phosphorous signature. The source of these phosphorus and sulphur in the larval body, is may be from the denaturation of cellular organelles along with sulphur containing protein and from nucleic acid due to degradation of enzymes (Jiang et al., 2015).

Polymer derived nanocarbon induced dead larvae were separated and examined under microscope. The microscopic images were depicted in Fig. S2 and S3. Fig. S2 and S3 clearly highlighted that the black nanocarbon were deposited on the larval body surface and make them uncomfortable to showed their normal behaviour. Body surface also damaged by these nanocarbon and ingestion of same through mouth leads to complete blockage and causes cellular damage of alimentary canal which may inhibit their normal feeding habit and exhibited abnormal behaviour with respect to wriggling activity. However, control larvae do not indicate such deformations (Fig. S2-S3). Fig. S3 also indicates that the larval body becomes radish to radish brown appearance with broken gut and alimentary canal, ruptured body surface under exposure of nanocarbon from PET, HDPE, LDPE, and PP. Moreover, other visual changes such as shrinking of larval body, disorganised gut etc. was also observed under the influence of nanocarbon from PET, LDPE, PP and PS for both third and fourth instar larvae. Present observations are very much consistent with the earlier study (Rodrígueze et al., 2022; Shaw et al., 2023). The death of larvae under the influence of nanocarbon may be due to its direct penetration into the larval body or by ingestion, oxidative stress from reactive oxygen species, DNA and chromosomal denaturation, rupture of larval body and membrane damage are may be the main causes for disturbance in proton motive force that decrease the membrane potentiality and permeability. All these are collectively responsible for malfunctioning of cell that leads to cell death (Shaw et al., 2023; Hajra and Mondal, 2016).

Nanocarbon treated larvae were examined through histological study and results depicted in Fig. 5 . Microscopic observation from Fig. 5 showed presence of swollen cell, loss of cellular integrity in midgut of both 3rd and 4th instar larvae. Damaged membrane and nucleus with cell detachment from basal lamina with loss of cytoplastic and nuclear materials was also observed. Epithelial cell from midgut region was also damaged under all nanocarbon exposed larvae (Fig. 5). Moreover, other changes such as cellular damage, gastric caeca burst, damaged microvilli etc. was also recorded. Similar cellular damages of mosquito larvae under the exposure of carbon dot-Ag nanohybrid was reported by earlier researchers Sultana et al. (2018).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of six different PDNCMs on gram seedlings

ce:section.ce:para: Seedling growth parameters such as root length and shoot length, number of lateral roots, and seedling biomass were recorded under different plastic origin carbon treatments and the results are presented in Table S2. From the Table S2, it is clear that root length was increased significantly (p < 0.001) under all treatments as compared to control. Total number of lateral roots was also significantly (p < 0.005) increased under the PET, HDPE, PVC and LDPE origin nanocarbon treatments. Similarly, shoot length was also increased under all treatments and it was significant (p < 0.001) under PET, HDPE, PVC and LDPE treatments with compared to control. Increased fresh and dry biomass were also recorded under all PDNCMs treatments with compared to control. Fresh biomass was increased by order as LDPE> PVC > HDPE> PP > PS > PET treatment, while PVC > PP > HDPE> LDPE> PET> PS for dry biomass. Carbon nanomaterials mediated improved nutrient uptake and nutrient use efficiency might be responsible for enhanced growth of gram seedlings under different PDNCMs treatments (Zhao et al., 2021). Seedling growth promotion could be attributed due the overexpression of aquaporin and increased water uptake under the interaction of nanomaterials (Mahakham et al., 2017; Servin et al., 2015). Almost similar improvement of corn seedlings in presence of carbon nanoparticles was reported by previous literature Zhao et al. (2021). Earlier researchers Sonkar et al. (2012) also highlighted the increased growth and subsequently enhancement of biomass accumulation in gram seedlings under the treatment of water-soluble carbon nanomaterials.

The photosynthetic pigments such as chlorophyll a and b, and carotenoids were measured under different treatments and presented in Fig. 6 (A, B) and Table S2. Results revealed that chlorophyll a and b were significantly (p < 0.01) improved under all PDNCMs treatments as compared control. However, no significant change was recorded for carotenoids accumulation in leaves. On other hand, primary metabolites such as carbohydrate and protein contents were enhanced in gram seedlings under all treatments over control, except at PVC treatment, which showed declined carbohydrate accumulation in gram seedlings (Table S2). Almost similar improvement of chlorophyll content in mung bean was also reported by earlier researchers Shekhawat et al. (2021) and demonstrated the increased chlorophyll biosynthesis could be responsible for higher accumulation of chlorophyll under the influence of carbon nanoparticles. Higher photosynthetic pigments are directly correlated with increased accumulation of photosynthate in plants (Mahakham et al., 2016). Therefore, increased carbohydrate content in gram seedlings under different PDNCMs treatments probably due to increased photosynthetic pigments and higher activity of photosynthesis. Earlier researchers Shekhawat et al. (2021) also reported the similar impacts of carbon nanoparticles on accumulation of both sugar and protein contents in mung bean seedlings.

Evaluation the level of MDA, a product of membrane lipid peroxidation is the good indicator of oxidative stress in plants (Mondal, 2017). In present study, the MDA level in gram seedlings was decreased significantly (p < 0.001) under all PDNCMs treatments as compared with control (Fig. 6c and Table S2). However, antioxidant status such as proline content (non-enzymatic) and catalase activity (enzymatic) were estimated and depicted in Fig. 6c and Table S2. From the Fig. 6c, it is clear that proline accumulation was reduced under almost all treatments with respect to control, except the treatment of PVC. In addition, catalase activity was also decreased significant (p < 0.001) under all PDNCMs treatments. Reduced oxidative stress and subsequently lower accumulation of MDA in peanut upon exposure of carbon dots was also reported by earlier researchers (Su et al., 2018). Reduced accumulation of proline and catalase activity in gram seedlings might be due to decreased oxidative stress generated in presence of nanocarbon. Almost similar reduction of plant antioxidants status under low level of oxidative stress was demonstrated by previous literature (Yuan et al., 2021).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of different PDNCMs on anatomical structures of gram seedlings
ce:section.ce:para: Anatomical structures of both shoot and root of gram seedlings grown under different PDNCMs treatments were photographed under microscopic study and are presented in Fig. 6. From the Fig. 6, it is clear that all tissue systems such as epidermis, cortex and vascular bundle were developed properly and no detrimental changes in anatomical structures of both root and shoot were recorded under the effect of different nanocarbon treatments. However, well developed vascular tissues in root were recorded under all PDNCMs treatments as contrast to control. Additionally, diameter of xylem tissue was also recorded as wider under all PDNCMs treatments over control, except PET and LDPE. Similarly, improved cortical tissue and pith of stem were also improved as compared with control. However, decreased cortical zone was recorded under the influence of LDPE and PP-based nanocarbon treatments. The positive impact of nanocarbon on tissue differentiation conceivably attributed to improved tissue organisation in gram seedlings. Improved water conduction from medium to root and then root to shoot under the influence of different nanocarbon derived from plastics might be responsible for wider and well-developed xylem in gram seedlings. Improved cell wall and tissue system in plants under influence of different nanoparticles were previously reported by several researchers (Anand et al., 2021; Gandhi et al., 2021).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of six different PDNCMs on mitotic cells of Allium cepa
ce:section.ce:para: Study of mitotic index is one of the reliable approaches to assess the toxicity of any substances and it usually measures the total number of cells undergo division of a cell cycle (Shaw et al., 2023). In present study, effects of six different PDNCs on mitotic cells of root tip of onion bulb (Allium cepa) were recorded under different exposure time and are presented in Table S3 (a-d) and Fig. 7 . Results revealed that mitotic index was declined under all PDNCMs treatments after both 3 and 7 days (separately in two experimental set up) of exposure time, over control. Highest decrease in mitotic index was recorded under PS-based nanocarbon treatment, while HDPE-based nanocarbon showed lowest decrease in mitotic index. However, change in mitotic index under PDNCMs treatments were negligible with respect to control. Additionally, no substantial decrease in mitotic index was also recorded with increased exposure time. However, several chromosomal abnormalities were recorded under six different nanocarbon treatments after both 3 and 7 days (separately) of exposure time and are depicted in Table S (3a-d) and Fig. 7. Results revealed that abnormality index was increased under all plastic derived nanocarbon treatments, as compared with control. Several chromosomal abnormalities including sticky metaphase, diagonal metaphase, C-metaphase, diagonal anaphase, anaphase bridge and vagrant chromosome were recorded under different nanocarbon treatments and diagonal metaphase, diagonal anaphase and vagrant chromosome were more common under both duration of treatment exposure. However, abnormality index was very much similar with control. Additionally, no substantial change in abnormality index and percentage of different abnormal cells were recorded with increased time of treatments exposure. Very recently, Shaw et al. (2023) reported similar effects of different plastic origin carbon nanocomposites on mitotic cells of Allium cepa. Negligible change in both mitotic index and abnormality index of root tip cells in presence of plastic origin carbon dots (10–30 nm) was also demonstrated in Allium cepa by earlier researchers Chaudhary et al. (2021).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of six different PDNCMs on Poecilia reticulata (guppy fish)

ce:section.ce:para: Poecilia reticulata were fed with nanocarbon materials treated larvae each day on an average of five larvae showed a high feeding activity against filaria vectors and also showed normal physiological activity. No significant behavioural abnormalities were observed during the experimental time period.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Estimation of muscle glucose and protein
ce:section.ce:para: A slight increment (0.48, 0.14, 1.7, and 0.15 % respectively) of glucose level in the muscle of experimental aliquot was observed in PET, HDPE, PVC and PP origin nanocarbon treated larvae, fed guppy fishes in comparison to the control (21.63 mg/dL). However, slightly decreased to 1.02 % and 0.94 % in PS and LDPE treated larvae feeding guppy fish, respectively. Under aerobic conditions, glucose is catabolized through the metabolic pathway to generate energy and this energy will be utilized for physical activity and reproduction. Glucose may also enter in pentose phosphate pathway leading to the production of NADPH for lipid biosynthesis purposes and ribose 5-phosphate required for nucleotide synthesis. Excess glucose may be stored as glycogen (glycogenesis) for future perspective or converted to lipids to maintained glucose homeostasis as well as maintained normal metabolic process (Enes et al., 2008). On other hand, the amount of protein in control set guppy fish was 8.57 g/dL. Whereas, the amount of protein in fishes fed with PET, HDPE, PVC, LDPE, PP and PS origin carbon nano treated larvae were 8.67 g/dL, 8.95 g/dL, 9.23 g/dL, 8.44 g/dL, 8.53 g/dL and 8.61 g/dL respectively. Noticeably, amount of protein increased 7.7 % in guppy fish fed with PVC nanocarbon treated larvae. The increased level of protein content in fish muscle tissues reflects the balance between the synthesis of protein within the tissue and its gradual degradation, which seems to be a good indication of a better metabolism rate (Gabriel et al., 2010). The lower rate of metabolism in the muscle tissues discouraged the fish species from judicious intake of foods, thus there was a greater possibility of storage of energy due to less breakdown of accrued protein content, mainly in the muscle tissue to maintain the equilibrium of the protein concentration in the plasma during protein deficiency (Das et al., 2023).

All fishes fed with six different plastics derived nanocarbon treated mosquito larvae during ten-day experiment showed normal blood erythrocytes except guppies fed with PVC nanocarbon treated larvae and the results were represent in Fig. 8 . Fishes fed with PVC nanocarbon treated larvae showed very little number of elongated erythrocytes, this may be due to presence of chlorine in PVC origin nanocarbon or some oxidative stress. However, guppies fed with PET, HDPE, LDPE, PP and PS derived nanocarbon treated larvae displayed normal erythrocytes and other blood cells. They showed normal matured erythrocytes along with normal nucleus with regular size and shape (Das et al., 2021).

our research clearly illustrate that thermally fabricated waste plastic origin carbonaceous nano materials have great advantages over chemical and physical methods, because of its low cost, single step, solvent and high energy free synthesis process. Various mosquito control tools such as transgenic mosquito, sterile insect technique, different biological method etc. are rampantly used but none of them can give fully satisfied results. Due to high resistance nature of mosquito larvae, against different chemical insecticides, novel eco-friendly mosquito controlling method are highly desirable. Thus, alternative ecofriendly mosquito controlling methods such as use of carbonaceous nano materials may be one of them. This study highlighted that PDNCMs aqueous solutions showed improve larval mortality and developmental retardation rate with increasing concentrations. PDNCMs caused cell damage, alimentary canal deposition through feeding and depositions on siphon leads to unproper respiration. Damaging of body as well as rupturing of chitin layer was also observed. Present research also revelled the in-depth information of PDNCMs impact on non-target organism (Cicer arietinum, Allium cepa and Poecilia reticulata). For the first time, entire biochemical and stress parameters with anatomical and histological assessment of Cicer arietinum plants exposure with nanocarbon treatment were performed and result gives positive responses. Cytotoxicity study of Allium cepa (root tips chromosome) showed no remarkable damaging properties on the cellular level. There is very little or nothing information available regarding guppy fish metabolism and blood erythrocytes status after feeding of nanocarbon treated mosquito larvae, this study revealed no significant changes occurred in guppy fish after feeding of nanocarbon treated larvae.

Future research also sheds light on the impacts of PDNCMs on the behaviours, stress parameters of different aquatic organism and possible toxicity on aquatic ecosystem. The major limitation of this research is the lack of in-depth investigation and analysis on molecular mechanism of the mortality of mosquito larvae influenced by polymer origin nanocarbon. Moreover, the larvicide activity of nanocarbon was only focused on the third and fourth instar larvae. However, in future, the effectiveness of the same material could be check with eggs, 1st and 2nd instar larvae of different mosquito vectors. Again, future research should be under taken on the stability and activity of nanocarbon particles with and without application of various capping agent. Similarly, the impact of plastic origin nanocarbon could be check with respect to its toxicity and accumulation in different aquatic non-target organism.

Present research highlighted in the area of formation of nanocarbon particles from waste plastics and same was used in the field of mosquito larva control. Previous literature is very scanty where synthesized carbon nano was used to kill the mosquito larvae. Very restricted research or almost nothing was available based on histopathological investigation of carbon nano treatment synthesized from six different waste plastics against mosquito larvae and on Cicer arietinum anatomical and histological study of xylem, phloem. Moreover, this paper also specifically focused on the in-depth investigation of larval mortality and developmental abnormalities and also evaluation the impact of carbon nano on the non-target organism, Cicer arietinum and Allium cepa. There is very little or nothing information available regarding guppy fish metabolism and blood erythrocytes status after feeding of nanocarbon treated mosquito larvae and this study revealed new highlights regarding guppy fish metabolism and erythrocytes status.

All applicable international and national guidelines for the care and use of animals were followed. All procedures performed in studies involving animals were in accordance with the ethical standards of the institution or practice at which the studies were conducted.

Vikky Shaw: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Rajesh Koley: Methodology, Formal analysis. Sugata Das: Methodology, Formal analysis. Tulika Saha: Methodology, Formal analysis. Naba Kumar Mondal: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
